title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text: Welcome to the ship’s training manual for digital media consumers.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
1 Navigating the Fragmented Archipelago
In this lesson we sail through the scattered seas of paid media platforms.

The Fragmented Map
The modern streaming landscape is no longer a simple, unified ocean. In the early days of digital distribution, services like Netflix and Spotify offered consumers a single, centralized library for a reasonable monthly fee. That initial promise has vanished. Today, original entertainment is scattered across dozens of isolated digital islands. Major media conglomerates have withdrawn their properties from shared services to build their own exclusive platforms (Leskovšek 2024). Manga, music, television series, and feature films are split across a dizzying array of competing portals.
This corporate balkanization has created a severe burden. To watch a basic selection of popular shows or read their favorite serialized manga, fans are forced to maintain multiple simultaneous subscriptions (Priancha and Khairunnisa 2024). Managing these accounts is exhausting. Consumers face an overwhelming cognitive load as they navigate separate billing cycles, user interfaces, and login credentials. This phenomenon is often termed subscription fatigue.
The financial reality of this fragmentation is stark. Because each digital service charges its subscription fee independently of its competitors, the cumulative cost of legal access rises rapidly. For lower-earning consumers, students, or families living in developing economies, a complete subscription stack is simply unaffordable (Leskovšek 2024). They are priced out of the legitimate market. When legal options are economically impossible, many consumers find themselves drawn back to the unofficial channels of digital piracy. They resort to downloading or streaming media from unauthorized vendors who charge little or nothing for access.
Consumers will now be boarded onto the pirate ship.


Pirate Ship Perspective
Pirates argue that this commercial fragmentation directly encourages illicit downloading. When official channels fail to provide a simple, centralized gateway, consumers look for a single unified route. Pirate websites and illegal streaming aggregators succeed because they solve this search and convenience friction. They bring the entire scattered archipelago back into a single, easily accessible harbor.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
2 Managing the Ship’s Coinpurse
A pirate ship needs a budget. This section explores how piracy is often justified as a money-saving measure.

When Cost Matters
Cost is a powerful driver of human behavior. For the average consumer, turning to digital piracy is frequently a rational, utility-maximizing economic decision. When household budgets are tight, paying for premium software licenses and streaming subscriptions is difficult to justify. Many consumers view piracy as a necessary budget-saving strategy, while still participating in contemporary cultural life.
The financial calculation is highly relative. Until the cost of legal media becomes negligible compared to a person’s monthly disposable income, unauthorized downloading remains highly attractive (Leskovšek 2024). This disparity is especially clear among low-income brackets, students, and regions where purchasing power parity is not reflected in regional pricing models. For instance, a student who wants access to a vast, costly music library or a reader following dozens of serialized manga series may find the cumulative cost of legal purchases completely prohibitive. For them, piracy feels like the only viable path to access.


Economic Pressure
Digital piracy does not exist in a vacuum. It acts as a powerful economic competitor. Gabe Newell, the co-founder of Valve, famously observed that piracy is almost always a service problem rather than a pricing problem. If a legal provider offers a product instantly, reliably, and in a convenient format, consumers are more likely to pay for it. He proved this theory by introducing Steam to the Russian market. Many industry analysts believed Russia was a lost cause due to rampant piracy, but Steam became a massive commercial success there because it outcompeted illicit sites on convenience, localization, and ease of use.
Therefore, piracy exerts a vital downward price pressure on the legitimate market. It acts as an invisible competitor that forces media companies to keep their prices reasonable and improve their user experiences. When pirate sites offer a superior, friction-free experience, legal merchants must innovate to survive.
However, this economic feedback loop is not a simple justification for every individual choice. While piracy can drive positive market reforms, it also risks starving smaller, independent creators who lack the resources to survive even minor revenue losses.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
3 Smuggling Truth & Ancient Archives
This section looks at piracy as a way of preserving content and granting access to censored media.

Archiving Forgotten Work
The preservation of digital culture is currently in a state of crisis. A landmark 2023 study by the Video Game History Foundation (Video Game History Foundation 2023) revealed a shocking reality: approximately 87 percent of classic video games released in the United States before 2010 are out of print. They are completely unavailable through legitimate commercial channels. When media companies delist classic games, remove digital movies from storefronts, or let older television shows sink into obscurity, legal consumers have no official way to purchase them.
In this hostile environment, pirate communities often perform the essential work of cultural preservation. They act as informal curators of human history. These groups locate, rip, and archive rare or forgotten media that would otherwise disappear forever into digital oblivion. The rise of shadow libraries like Anna’s Archive, Sci-Hub, and Library Genesis represents a parallel effort in the academic world. These platforms circumvent restrictive academic paywalls to make scientific knowledge freely available to researchers globally. For many preservationists, this form of unauthorized sharing is not a malicious act of theft, but a necessary campaign of cultural rescue.


Access to Censored and Free Media
The flow of information is highly restricted in many parts of the world. In nations like Russia and China, state authorities maintain a tight grip on internet traffic and official media distribution. They routinely modify, delay, or ban foreign art, literature, and news that conflicts with government narratives. In these heavily censored environments, unauthorized file-sharing networks become essential pipelines for truth.
Decentralized peer-to-peer protocols, such as BitTorrent, are incredibly difficult for governments to shut down. They allow citizens to bypass state-mandated firewalls and access uncensored, original versions of global media. For these populations, piracy is not merely a cheap way to watch movies, but an informal institution of freedom. It acts as a vital smuggling route for art, culture, and independent journalism. Yet, navigating these unregulated channels carries severe risks. Seeking access to forbidden media through illicit sources can expose users to intense state surveillance, heavy legal penalties, and dangerous cyber-threats.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
4 The Honest Merchant’s Toll
Now we shall explore arguments against piracy from the perspective of fairness and law.

Fairness for Legal Consumers
A shared marketplace relies on cooperative ethics to survive. When a consumer chooses to purchase a legitimate subscription, they enter into a social contract. They expect their financial contribution to support the creators and maintain the underlying infrastructure. However, this system relies on everyone pulling their own weight. When a large segment of the population chooses to pirate instead, the economic balance is disrupted. This creates a classic free-rider problem.
This disruption directly impacts law-abiding consumers. When piracy causes a significant loss in official revenue, legitimate media providers often respond by raising subscription fees or restricting features. Paying customers end up subsidizing the access of those who pirate for free. For many legal users, this feels deeply unfair. They are left holding the bill while others bypass the gate without paying. Ethical commerce requires a shared commitment to fairness, where all participants respect the rules of the marketplace.


Market Trust
Predictable revenue is the lifeblood of the creative industry. Legal media providers and independent studios depend on steady sales to plan future projects, pay their staff, and invest in risky artistic endeavors. Piracy actively erodes this market trust. When unauthorized downloading becomes normalized, it undermines the perceived value of digital goods. Consumers begin to view creative work as something that should inherently be free, which severely devalues the labor of creators.
This erosion of trust has a cascading effect on legitimate buyers. When honest consumers see that millions of people are accessing the same content for free with zero consequences, they begin to question their own choices. They may start to feel foolish for paying. This dynamic weakens the overall incentive to participate in the legal market, which can eventually destabilize the entire digital ecosystem. Without trust and mutual respect between buyers, sellers, and creators, the legal market cannot sustain itself.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
section: 5 Scurvy, Scams, and Software Sea Monsters
text:
5 Scurvy, Scams, and Software Sea Monsters
This lesson covers the risks of piracy, from malware to untrustworthy sites.

Unregulated Waters
The unofficial channels of digital piracy are highly dangerous territories. Legitimate platforms are subject to strict regulatory oversight, consumer protection laws, and corporate accountability, but pirate sites operate entirely outside the law. They are digital badlands. In these unregulated waters, consumers have zero legal protections or safety guarantees. Every click is a gamble.
The hazards on these platforms are frequent and severe. Visitors are routinely bombarded with malicious pop-up advertisements, deceptive download buttons, and fraudulent redirects. Cyber-criminals use these sites as primary distribution channels for digital infections. They often camouflage dangerous software as popular movies, television episodes, or game cracks. Unsuspecting users who are simply searching for a free download can easily find themselves trapped in a web of digital scams and malicious scripts.


Cost of Convenience
Pirating media may seem like an easy, cost-free shortcut, but the hidden expenses can be devastating. Visitors to unauthorized websites face an incredibly high risk of cyber-security compromise. Industry research and cybersecurity reports demonstrate that consumers are up to 65 times more likely to encounter malware on piracy sites than on legitimate web portals (Digital Citizens Alliance 2021). The “free” price tag of pirated content is a dangerous illusion.
The software sea monsters lurking on these platforms can cause permanent damage. Users routinely download Trojan horses, ransomware, and spyware that are disguised as media files. These infections can silently harvest personal logins, capture financial data, or lock down entire devices for extortion. In some cases, compromised computers are co-opted into massive botnets to launch distributed denial-of-service attacks. When a user chooses to pirate, they are often trading their digital safety and personal identity for a temporary discount.

title: Consumer Training: Media Access
text:
6 The Ghost Crew’s True Masters
This lesson examines why pirate sites themselves need income and what that means for the ecosystem.

Who Pays the Pirate Toll?
The popular image of the digital pirate is often that of a benevolent rebel or an idealistic digital Robin Hood. This is a myth. Operating a high-traffic file-sharing website or streaming portal is an expensive enterprise. These sites require massive server bandwidth, robust hosting infrastructure, and continuous technical maintenance to stay online. To cover these overhead costs and generate profit, pirate operators must establish reliable streams of income. They are commercial enterprises.
To generate revenue, these platforms employ highly aggressive, unregulated monetization models. They partner with shady third-party ad networks that specialize in high-risk advertising. Visitors are forced to navigate intrusive pop-under ads, deceptive betting promotions, and high-stakes cryptocurrency scams. Many of these sites also utilize silent cryptocurrency mining scripts that hijack a visitor’s computer processor to mine digital coins. The pirate toll is always paid. It is simply extracted from the user’s device security and attention rather than their credit card.


Side Effects of Pirate Funding
The financial scale of the digital piracy ecosystem is staggering. Globally, ad-supported piracy websites and illicit streaming apps generate more than 2.5 billion dollars in annual revenue (Digital Citizens Alliance 2021). This massive influx of money creates a powerful financial incentive for operators to keep their illegal operations online. It is a highly lucrative business model.
For the ethical consumer, this funding structure creates a profound moral conflict. Many users choose to pirate because they want to avoid supporting what they see as greedy media conglomerates, but their traffic directly enriches commercial criminal syndicates. These illicit profits do not support creative development or cultural preservation. Instead, they fund international criminal organizations that engage in identity theft, financial fraud, and wider cyber-crime networks. When a consumer visits a pirate site, they are actively funding a shadow industry that makes the entire internet less safe for everyone.

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text: Piracy is often talked about from the perspective of consumers, but creators sit at the ethical core of the discussion. Creators invest time, money and creative labor into their work and piracy directly affects the return on that investment. The ethical question from the creators’ perspective is: does the public’s interest in access outweigh the creator’s right to control and profit from their work? There are two main rights of the creators that piracy is challenging: economic rights (compensation for use, transferable) and moral rights (authorship and integrity, permanent). Both of these rights are recognized under the Berne Convention (World Intellectual Property Organization 1886).

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text: Piracy is often talked about from the perspective of consumers, but creators sit at the ethical core of the discussion. Creators invest time, money and creative labor into their work and piracy directly affects the return on that investment. The ethical question from the creators’ perspective is: does the public’s interest in access outweigh the creator’s right to control and profit from their work? There are two main rights of the creators that piracy is challenging: economic rights (compensation for use, transferable) and moral rights (authorship and integrity, permanent). Both of these rights are recognized under the Berne Convention (World Intellectual Property Organization 1886).

title: The Creator’s Perspective
text:
Ethical Frameworks
There are three important philosophical frameworks when looking at piracy from the perspective of the creator:

Locke’s labour theory: creators who invest labour in their work have a natural property right to it. Piracy takes that product without consent, which is a violation of their property right (Locke 1689).
Kantian deontology: piracy fails the universalisability test (if everyone pirated, creation would collapse) and treats creators as means rather than as agents whose rights deserve respect (UBC Allard School of Law – Social Media Law 2024).
Utilitarianism: copyright gives creators a limited monopoly to recoup their investment, which in theory funds future creation and maximises long-term benefit of both creators and consumers (UBC Allard School of Law – Social Media Law 2024).

title: The Creator’s Perspective
section: The Case Against Piracy: Harm to Creators
text:
The Case Against Piracy: Harm to Creators
One of the most famous creator-vs-piracy cases was Metallica v. Napster in 2000. Napster was a popular file sharing platform at the time and Metallica identified 335,435 users sharing their catalogue without consent and sued Napster into shutdown. This case is Kantian at its core: Napster used artists’ work as a means for profit without their consent. It is also Lockean in consequence: the product of creators’ labour was taken without compensation, a direct violation of their property right.

“[Napster] never asked us or other artists if we wanted to participate in their business”
— Lars Ulrich, US Senate testimony, 2000

The harm to creators extends beyond landmark legal cases. Waldfogel found that the number of new music releases did not decline even as revenue fell sharply after file-sharing became widespread (Waldfogel 2012). The reasoning behind this was that creators had already shifted to live performance, merchandise and licensing. These are sources of income that piracy does not directly affect. Spotify pays $0.003–$0.005 per stream ($3,000–$5,000 per million streams (Ditto Music 2024)), while a single band tee at $35 is worth the equivalent of approximately 9,000 streams (atVenu 2023). This directly challenges the utilitarian case for strict enforcement. The financial harm to sales is real, but the harm to creation itself is less obvious.
Another interesting question is whether the creator size and popularity affect the ethics of pirating. Pirating from a billion-dollar studio is structurally different from pirating from a two-person indie team. This viewpoint goes against Kantian ethics, where the wrongness of an act is independent of the target’s wealth.

title: The Creator’s Perspective
section: The Case For Piracy: Access and Distribution
text:
The Case For Piracy: Access and Distribution
Even though piracy can cause monetary harm to creators, not all creators oppose it either. When content is banned or refused classification, piracy becomes the only option. For example, the game Hotline Miami 2 (2015) was refused classification in Australia. After the news broke, the developer Jonatan Söderström publicly told Australian fans to “just pirate it” (Engadget 2015).
There are also many cases where digital rights management (DRM) punishes legal buyers more than pirates. CD Projekt Red removed all DRM from their games, arguing copy protection “makes the life of a legal consumer more difficult” while pirates bypass it on day one anyway (Lomas 2016). This is a utilitarian calculation: rather than restricting access, they chose to offer a better experience to paying customers and win over pirates through quality. Their flagship game, The Witcher 3, sold nearly 10 million copies without copy protection (Purchese 2016).

“We believe in the carrot, not the stick. We cannot force people to buy things – we can only convince them to do it.”
— Marcin Iwiński, co-founder of CD Projekt Red (Lomas 2016)

In both cases, the creators who embraced piracy – or at least refused to fight it – argued that a better product is a more effective response than restriction.

title: Digital Piracy
text:
CS-E5480 Digital Ethics·Group Presentation
    Digital Piracy
    An Ethical Examination
    
      Piracy resists easy moral judgment. Depending on whose interests are
      foregrounded — the consumer priced out of fragmented platforms, the creator
      whose livelihood depends on compensation, or the buyer who discovers their
      "purchase" was only ever a licence — the same act can appear as a rational
      response, an ethical violation, or a symptom of a broken system. These three
      perspectives do not converge on a verdict; they map the terrain of a genuinely
      contested problem.
    
  

  
    
      
      Anonymous, A Spanish Galleon, mid-17th century engraving. Public domain.
    
  




  ☠ Chart the Course ☠


Three Perspectives


  
    I
    The Consumer's Perspective
    How fragmented streaming landscapes and rising subscription costs reframe piracy as a rational — if not justifiable — choice.
    Set Sail →
  
  
    II
    The Creator's Perspective
    Economic and moral rights under the Berne Convention, and what three philosophical frameworks reveal about the ethics of unpaid use.
    Set Sail →
  
  
    III
    Ownership and Licensing
    When clicking "Buy" delivers a revocable licence instead of property, the ethical calculus of piracy shifts in ways the law has not caught up with.
    Set Sail →

title: Digital Ownership
text: ##This whole page is a placeholder generated by Claude

title: Digital Ownership
text:
The ethical question
When you pay full price to buy something digital, can you truly own it — or are you just renting access until the seller decides otherwise? This section examines the gap between what “purchasing” digital media promises and what it actually delivers, and why that gap matters for the ethics of piracy.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Core thesis
Clicking “Buy” on a digital storefront hands you a revocable licence, not a property right. The Federal Trade Commission warned in 2024 that the word buy can be actively misleading when the seller retains the right to take access away (FTC, 2024). Most consumers don’t realise the swap from ownership to licencing has happened.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Rent vs. own




Subscription
Digital “purchase”
Physical copy




Pay once?
No
Yes
Yes


Revocable?
Yes
Yes
No


Survives platform shutdown?
No
No
Yes


Resell/lend?
No
No
Yes



Subscriptions are at least honest — you pay to keep access. One-time digital purchases borrow ownership’s vocabulary and price point while delivering something much closer to a rental.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Platform dependency & lock-in

Your library is tied to one platform’s app and account
You cannot move it to a competitor or export it
You cannot resell or lend individual items
If you leave the platform (or the platform leaves you), the library goes with it

All of these were taken for granted with physical media.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
When platforms die: The Crew
Ubisoft’s racing game The Crew was sold as a purchase to millions of players. Because it required a persistent server connection even for solo play, when Ubisoft shut those servers off on 31 March 2024, the game became completely unplayable — not degraded, not offline-only, simply gone.
French consumer group UFC-Que Choisir and a California class action both sued Ubisoft, alleging buyers had been misled into thinking they owned the game (Game Developer, 2024). The case attracted backing from the Stop Killing Games initiative, which had gathered over 1.3 million signatures arguing that publishers should not be allowed to permanently disable purchased software (Wikipedia, 2024).

title: Digital Ownership
text:
The legal mechanism: first-sale doctrine
Copyright law was designed around physical copies and included a consumer-protective balance: the first-sale doctrine, which lets you resell, lend, or give away a copy you lawfully bought. That is what makes second-hand bookshops and libraries legal.
Digital distribution exploits the gap. In Capitol Records v. ReDigi (2018), courts held that first-sale doctrine does not extend to digital files — transferring a file necessarily creates a new copy, which is the copyright holder’s exclusive right. Sellers then layer licence agreements on top of that gap, stripping consumers of resale and transfer rights without ever announcing the loss.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Counter-arguments
“The terms are disclosed — buyers agreed.” EULA terms are buried, unreadable, and non-negotiable. Nominal consent to a 50-page agreement written by the seller’s lawyers is not meaningful informed consent.
“DRM and always-online protect creators from piracy.” That may justify technical measures — it does not justify labelling a revocable licence as a purchase.
“Digital is cheaper and more convenient.” Convenience is a genuine benefit. Mislabelling the product is a separate issue.
“Always-online enables updates and anti-cheat.” Those are design choices with trade-offs. The harm is not the design choice itself but selling the result as permanent ownership.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
Why it matters ethically

Honesty: consumers are charged ownership prices and given ownership language, but receive a revocable licence
Informed consent: the difference between what is sold and what is delivered is not communicated clearly
Preservation: when a paid-for library can be erased by a corporate decision, personal archiving starts to look less like theft and more like self-defence

This doesn’t make piracy morally right. But it does mean the moral weight that rights-holders invoke against piracy — the appeal to a fair exchange honestly entered into — is partly undermined by their own side of the bargain.

title: Digital Ownership
text:
References
Federal Trade Commission. (2024, April). Do you really own the digital items you paid for? https://consumer.ftc.gov/consumer-alerts/2024/04/do-you-really-own-digital-items-you-paid
Game Developer. (2024). French consumer group sues Ubisoft over shutdown of The Crew. https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/french-consumer-group-sues-ubisoft-over-shutdown-of-the-crew
Wikipedia. (2024). Stop Killing Games. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stop_Killing_Games
Wikipedia. (2018). Capitol Records, LLC v. ReDigi Inc. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Capitol_Records,_LLC_v._ReDigi_Inc.

title: Project Plan: Digital Ethics Essay on Piracy
text:
Arguments for piracy: - Wider (equal) access to content in restricted areas (games banned in Australia, other censored content) - Wider distribution of content
Arguments against piracy: - Creators lose income - Are not incentivised to create as much content




Arguments for piracy: - Means for obtaining original efforts (manga, Blood, Taylor Swift catalogue) - Saves money (until costs become negligible to income) - Content fragmented across multiple platforms - Access to censored content and free media (Russia and China) - Archiving and saving information so that it doesn’t get forgotten (Anna’s Archive)
Arguments against piracy: - Unfair to people who follow the law (paying streaming fees) - Unregulated and risky: viruses and scams - Piracy sites need income too - Promote gambling





Renting (subscription) vs. owning
Media platform dependency: the media you purchase is locked into the platform
If a platform shuts down, what happens to the media you “own”?
Stop Killing Games initiative: online games can’t be played when providers shut down servers

Wikipedia: Stop Killing Games







What aspects of a media piece can you copyright, and what makes it distinct enough to be copyrightable?

Reference video

Does the size of the creator affect the morality of pirating? Is it okay to pirate from commercially successful companies vs. indie studios? Where is the limit?

Declare any AI usage
